name: microservices-patterns
description: Design microservices architectures with service boundaries, event-driven communication, and resilience patterns. Use when building distributed systems, decomposing monoliths, or implementing microservices.
capabilities: Guide service decomposition by business capability and DDD bounded contexts
Design synchronous and asynchronous communication patterns between services
Implement distributed transactions using saga pattern with compensation logic
Apply resilience patterns including circuit breaker, retry with backoff, and bulkhead isolation
Structure API gateway patterns for routing, aggregation, and cross-cutting concerns
Plan data management strategies with database-per-service and eventual consistency
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 29 static analysis findings are false positives from markdown code examples in educational documentation. The skill contains only documentation files (SKILL.md and implementation-playbook.md) with Python code samples demonstrating microservices patterns. No executable code, scripts, or actual security risks present. The 'external_commands' and 'network' patterns detected are from code block delimiters and example URLs in instructional content.
valueStatement: Building distributed systems requires navigating complex trade-offs between service boundaries, communication patterns, and data consistency. This skill provides proven patterns and code examples for decomposing monoliths, implementing resilient inter-service communication, and managing distributed transactions effectively.
limitations: Does not write production-ready code without context about your specific technology stack
Cannot evaluate existing microservices architectures without access to codebase and documentation
Patterns may require adaptation based on your team's operational maturity and infrastructure capabilities
Does not replace the need for platform engineering and DevOps expertise in distributed systems

useCases.title: Decompose a Monolithic Application
useCases.description: Software architect planning to break down a large Rails monolith into focused services. Use this skill to identify bounded contexts, define service boundaries, and plan a strangler fig migration strategy.

useCases.title: Design Event-Driven Communication
useCases.description: Backend engineer implementing asynchronous messaging between services. Use this skill to choose between Kafka, RabbitMQ, or cloud-native messaging, design event schemas, and implement event handlers with proper error handling.

useCases.title: Implement Resilience Patterns
useCases.description: Platform engineer building reliability into service-to-service communication. Use this skill to apply circuit breakers, implement retry logic with exponential backoff, and prevent cascade failures in your distributed system.

promptTemplates.title: Identify Service Boundaries
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this monolithic application [describe domain and modules] and suggest microservice boundaries. Group related functionality by business capability and DDD subdomains. Explain the reasoning for each proposed service boundary.
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting microservices decomposition from a monolith

promptTemplates.title: Choose Communication Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: I need [Service A] to communicate with [Service B] for [specific use case]. Compare synchronous REST vs asynchronous event-driven approaches. Recommend the best pattern considering latency, consistency requirements, and failure handling.
promptTemplates.scenario: Deciding between sync and async communication

promptTemplates.title: Design Saga for Distributed Transaction
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a saga orchestration for [business process involving multiple services]. Identify each transaction step, define compensating actions for failures, and show the execution flow with error handling.

promptTemplates.title: Apply Circuit Breaker Pattern
promptTemplates.prompt: My service calls [external service/dependency] and experiences intermittent failures. Design a circuit breaker implementation with appropriate failure threshold, recovery timeout, and fallback behavior. Show code in [your preferred language].
promptTemplates.scenario: Adding resilience to fragile service dependencies

bestPractices: Start with a modular monolith and extract services only when you have a clear business boundary and operational need
Design services around business capabilities and bounded contexts from domain-driven design, not technical layers
Prefer asynchronous event-driven communication over synchronous REST for loose coupling and better resilience
Implement circuit breakers, retries with exponential backoff, and timeouts for all inter-service calls
Apply database-per-service pattern to avoid shared database coupling and enable independent deployments
Use distributed tracing and structured logging to observe requests across service boundaries
antiPatterns: Distributed monolith - services that must be deployed together and share databases, defeating microservices benefits
Chatty services - excessive synchronous calls between services that create tight coupling and latency
Shared databases - multiple services accessing the same database creates schema lockstep and deployment dependencies
Ignoring failure modes - assuming networks are reliable, services are always available, or messages are guaranteed to deliver
Premature microservices - starting a new project with microservices before understanding the domain and operational burden

faq.question: When should I use microservices instead of a monolith?
faq.answer: Use microservices when you have clear domain boundaries, multiple teams needing independent deployment, and operational maturity to handle distributed complexity. Stay with a monolith for small teams, simple domains, or when starting a new product.

faq.question: How do I handle transactions across multiple services?
faq.answer: Use the saga pattern for distributed transactions. Each service performs a local transaction and publishes an event. If any step fails, execute compensating transactions in reverse order to roll back. Accept eventual consistency rather than ACID guarantees across services.

faq.question: Should I use REST or messaging for service communication?
faq.answer: Use synchronous REST when you need an immediate response and strong consistency. Use asynchronous messaging (events) when multiple services need to react to changes and you can accept eventual consistency. Prefer messaging for loose coupling and better resilience.

faq.question: How do I prevent cascade failures in microservices?
faq.answer: Implement circuit breakers that stop calling failing services after a threshold, retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures, request timeouts, and bulkhead patterns to isolate resource pools. Always design for graceful degradation when dependencies are unavailable.

faq.question: What is the biggest mistake teams make with microservices?
faq.answer: Starting with microservices without understanding the domain or having operational readiness. This creates distributed monoliths with all the complexity of distributed systems and none of the benefits. Begin with a well-structured modular monolith and extract services when you have clear boundaries.

faq.question: How do I debug issues across multiple services?
faq.answer: Implement distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Zipkin) to follow requests across service boundaries. Use correlation IDs in all logs and events. Centralize logs with structured logging. Set up proper observability with metrics and dashboards before you need them.
